frameworks.name: Bayesian optimal observer
frameworks.one_line: well-defined complex optimal rule for a perceptual decision with sensory uncertainty

frameworks.name: simple heuristic rules
frameworks.one_line: rule-based suboptimal strategies hypothesized as cognitively cheaper substitutes

frameworks.name: complex suboptimal rules
frameworks.one_line: non-optimal rules that closely mimic the optimal rule's predictions

frameworks.name: signal-detection / perceptual decision making
frameworks.one_line: task framework with sensory noise driving categorical choice

core_question: Within a single perceptual decision task, do humans behave near-optimally or follow simple heuristic rules?
key_assumptions: optimal decision rule is well-defined and complex in this task
simple suboptimal rules make qualitatively distinct predictions from the optimal rule
sensory uncertainty can be implicitly represented by observers
manipulations: comparison of optimal vs multiple simple suboptimal rules within one task
presence vs absence of trial-by-trial feedback
inclusion of complex suboptimal rules that approximate the optimal rule
key_findings: all tested simple rules fail to describe human behavior
the optimal rule accounts well for the data
several complex suboptimal rules are indistinguishable from the optimal one
the optimal model still describes behavior best without feedback
interpretations: observers behave near-optimally and implicitly represent sensory uncertainty
the optimal model is close to the true model in this perceptual task
limitations_noted: complex suboptimal rules cannot be distinguished from the optimal rule
